Are you passionate about AI ethics, digital policy, or social media safety?"},{"title":"Call for Fellowship in AI Ethics 2026–2027","url":"https://www.facebook.com/YOpportunities/posts/call-for-fellowship-in-ai-ethics-20262027-harvard-university-greece-the-center-f/1339648094857737/","text":"This fellowship focuses on the ethical, societal, and philosophical implications of artificial intelligence as it increasingly shapes everyday life. Fellows"},{"title":"From Code to Conscience: An Ethical Framework for ...","url":"https://www.ethics.harvard.edu/news/2025/11/code-conscience-ethical-framework-healthcare-ai-0","text":"This is not a hypothetical challenge; AI is already shaping clinical decisions, patient interactions and workforce management making ethical oversight not just optional, but a core responsibility of every healthcare board. To help boards navigate these challenges, Saviano presented a comprehensive AI governance framework rooted in ethics, developed through his research at Harvard’s Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics. Perhaps the most powerful aspect for healthcare boards is the framework’s concept of ‘boundaries of tolerance’, explicitly defining the ethical guardrails for an organization’s AI strategy. The framework helps boards and senior management teams move beyond vague principles to explicitly define their tolerance thresholds across each pillar of AI governance. 1. **Engage the board and senior management** – Apply the first two pillars of the BoT framework to more precisely articulate the ethical boundaries within their organization. In healthcare, where what breaks might be trust, safety or lives, an ethical AI governance framework offers a path that honors both innovation and responsibility, a path every healthcare board should now consider."},{"title":"Fellowships Overview","url":"https://www.mountaintop.international/fellowships","text":"July 12, 2025 - July 23, 2025. The two-week Leadership Institute will be held in 2025 at Harvard's Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics. Mountaintop covers"},{"title":"News & Events | Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics","url":"https://www.ethics.harvard.edu/pages/news-events","text":"Greene, Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics Faculty Associate since 2015, has been named Harvard's first Alfred and Rebecca Lin Professor of Civil Discourse. Current Undergraduate Fellow at the Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics Yael Goldstein is one of six Harvard College seniors who have won Rhodes Scholarships for 2026. Professor Jason D'Cruz, a current Civil Discourse Fellow at the ELSCE, recently published an article titled \"What Does the Trust Game Measure?\" in the Journal of Business Ethics. Jan-Paul Sandmann , current Graduate Fellow at the Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics, has just published a new article in Philosophy & Social Criticism . The Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics is proud to share that Craig McFarland ('25), a former Undergraduate Fellow and founder of the Undergraduate Ethics Society, has been named a Harvard-UK Fellow and Clarendon Scholar."},{"title":"About Us - Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics","url":"https://www.ethics.harvard.edu/about-us","text":"The Edmond & Lily Safra Center for Ethics responds to these challenges by preparing teachers, scholars, and public leaders to confront moral questions in real-world contexts. As the need for leaders who can make sound moral judgments in public and professional life increases, the wisdom of establishing a Center with the mission of promoting ethics teaching and research is more apparent today than ever. Faculty affiliates and fellows were encouraged to launch their own initiatives, supported by Center-hosted one-day workshops, multi-stage gatherings, and university-wide conferences that brought ethics into conversation with emerging challenges across disciplines. The Center’s fellowship programs continue to anchor its intellectual community, bringing together undergraduate and graduate students, postdoctoral scholars, and faculty from across disciplines to pursue research on urgent ethical questions. Under Beerbohm’s direction, the Center continues to be where ethics meets the world—supporting scholars and practitioners committed to navigating the complexities of public life with integrity, imagination, and democratic purpose."},{"title":"Applications are now open for the Fellowship in AΙ Ethics School and Stanford Engineering, awards annual “Magic Grants” to projects across both campuses that blend journalistic insight with technical innovation – from new tools for creators to technologies designed in the public interest. “You can’t talk about technical innovation without mentioning AI – and that’s especially true in journalism,” said Mark Hansen, professor of journalism at Columbia University and East Coast Director of the Brown Institute. “I am especially excited for this year’s projects, as they cover a broad diversity of topics in media, from developing new approaches to collecting information, analyzing it, and synthesizing it into stories told through text, audio, and video,” said Maneesh Agrawala, professor of computer science at Stanford Engineering and the West Coast Director of the Brown Institute. *Sally Herships, Director of Audio Program (Columbia Journalism School); Mark Fiore, Independent Visual Journalist and ’24 Knight Journalism Fellow (Stanford); perspectives are systematically under- or over-represented across online communities. *Established in 2012, the David and Helen Gurley Brown Institute is a collaboration between Columbia University and Stanford University, designed to encourage and support new endeavors in media innovation. At Stanford, the primary focus is on media technology, and the Institute is anchored in the School of Engineering.
